text: In this video, we present a case of fully robotic malignant retroperitoneal lymph node dissection with three-D modeling, following curative resection of colorectal cancer. Using three-D slicer, standard patient CT imaging was three-D reconstructed. The node and aorta were highlighted and superimposed on the three-D reconstruction.

The DaVinci XI surgical system's TilePro input port displays the three-dimensional modeling during the procedure. The patient is positioned in modified lithotomy, and ureteric stents are visibly inserted.

Under visual guidance, four robotic ports and one assistant port are inserted. Dense adhesions of the small bowel, resulting from the patient's previous anterior resection, are encountered and carefully divided. The bowel loops are then reflected superiorly.
The surgeon dissects the parietal peritoneum over the right common iliac artery, while identifying and preserving the left common iliac artery and both ureters.

The surgeon continues dissection after the aortic bifurcation using a monopolar energy device. A malignant lymph node is identified at the aortic bifurcation and sharply dissected, with guidance from live intraoperative referencing to the three-dimensional reconstruction.
The surgeon utilizes the three-dimensional reconstruction to establish a surgical plane between the malignant node and the anterior aortic wall, navigating through dense fibrotic tissue.

The specimen was removed in an endo-catch bag. The total operative time was a hundred and fifty minutes and estimated blood loss was less than fifty minutes. There were no post-operative complications.
